quickStart.title: Get productive in 30 seconds
steps: Install OpenClaw, then run: openclaw onboard --install-daemon
Check the gateway is alive: openclaw gateway status
Open the fastest first chat surface: openclaw dashboard
If you want chat apps next, add Telegram or Discord first (fastest bot-token flows), then pair your own DM
Run openclaw doctor after setup or upgrades to catch config drift, bad auth, and stale service state

dailyWorkflows.title: Fastest first chat without any messaging app
dailyWorkflows.description: Skip channel setup entirely. Use the local Control UI in your browser to confirm the gateway, model auth, and workspace all work before touching Telegram/Discord/Signal.

dailyWorkflows.title: Set up a new agent
dailyWorkflows.description: Create an isolated brain with its own workspace, sessions, persona files, and auth profiles. Best pattern when you want separate assistants for coding, ops, research, or family use.

dailyWorkflows.title: Check whether everything is healthy
dailyWorkflows.description: Use this before debugging. It catches broken services, invalid config, risky DM policies, orphan session files, cron schema drift, and missing memory-search prerequisites.

dailyWorkflows.title: See live runtime and channel health
dailyWorkflows.description: Shows whether the gateway is reachable, whether channels are connected, and recent session/link health. Add --deep when you suspect a channel auth or connectivity issue.

dailyWorkflows.title: Add Telegram fast
dailyWorkflows.description: Telegram is usually the easiest real chat channel: create a bot in BotFather, add the token, start the gateway, then approve your first DM pairing code.

dailyWorkflows.title: Add Discord for workspace-style use
dailyWorkflows.description: Best when you want multiple persistent topic channels. Create a bot, enable Message Content Intent, add it to your server, then allowlist the guild/channels you want.

dailyWorkflows.title: Connect Signal privately
dailyWorkflows.description: Use a separate Signal number or linked Signal device via signal-cli. Signal is great for private DM access but has more setup friction than Telegram/Discord.

dailyWorkflows.title: Inspect which skills are actually ready
dailyWorkflows.description: Don’t guess. This shows which skills are available and which are blocked by missing binaries, env vars, or dependencies.

dailyWorkflows.title: Search long-term memory
dailyWorkflows.description: Useful when the assistant has accumulated notes over time. Memory lives in Markdown files in the workspace, not hidden magic.

dailyWorkflows.title: Schedule a real recurring job
dailyWorkflows.description: Use cron for exact-time reminders, reports, and isolated background jobs. Prefer isolated cron for noisy or model-specific tasks.
dailyWorkflows.command: openclaw cron add --name "Morning brief" --cron "0 7 * * *" --session isolated --message "Summarize overnight updates" --announce

powerMoves.title: Agent army by channel/account binding
powerMoves.description: Run multiple isolated agents behind one gateway, then route inbound messages by channel, account, guild, group, topic, or even specific sender. This is the core pattern for a personal fleet: one coding agent, one alerts agent, one family agent, one social agent.
powerMoves.command: openclaw agents add coding && openclaw agents add alerts && openclaw agents list --bindings
powerMoves.why: You get separate workspaces, memory, auth, and sessions without running multiple gateways. It scales cleanly and prevents context bleed.

powerMoves.title: Use dashboard first, channels second
powerMoves.description: Bring the gateway up and confirm the local browser UI works before spending time on bot tokens, QR pairing, or Signal setup. If the dashboard works, your core install is fine.
powerMoves.why: This removes 80% of setup ambiguity. You separate gateway/model problems from channel problems immediately.

powerMoves.title: Secure multi-user inboxes with per-sender DM isolation
powerMoves.description: Set session.dmScope to per-channel-peer (or per-account-channel-peer in multi-account setups) so different people don’t silently share the same DM session history.
powerMoves.why: This is one of the highest-value safety settings. Shared main-session DMs are convenient for one owner, but risky for shared inboxes.

powerMoves.title: Heartbeat for awareness, cron for precision
powerMoves.description: Put recurring awareness checks into heartbeat, and exact-time tasks into cron. Example: inbox/calendar monitoring in heartbeat; daily 7am briefing and one-shot reminders in cron.
powerMoves.command: openclaw cron add --name "Reminder" --at "20m" --session main --system-event "Reminder text" --wake now
powerMoves.why: This keeps token use low while still giving you reliable scheduled actions. Heartbeat batches; cron pinpoints.

powerMoves.title: Thread-bind subagents in Discord/Telegram
powerMoves.description: Use /focus, /agents, /subagents, and ACP thread binding so a discussion thread becomes a dedicated workstream tied to one subagent or harness session.
powerMoves.why: This turns chat threads into durable task lanes instead of one noisy shared conversation.

powerMoves.title: Direct config surgery instead of manual JSON editing
powerMoves.description: Use config get/set/unset/validate for repeatable, scriptable changes. It’s much safer than hand-editing a big JSON5 file, especially remotely.
powerMoves.command: openclaw config validate && openclaw config set channels.telegram.linkPreview false
powerMoves.why: You get validation, automation, and less typo-driven downtime.

powerMoves.title: Treat doctor as the safe update command
powerMoves.description: After updates or weird behavior, run doctor before chasing ghosts. It repairs service/config mismatches and warns about insecure or outdated patterns.
powerMoves.why: Most production pain is boring drift: env overrides, stale services, missing deps, migrated config keys. Doctor is designed for exactly that.

gotchas.problem: You set up a channel but messages still do nothing.
gotchas.solution: Check openclaw gateway status, then openclaw channels status --probe, then openclaw logs --follow. Most failures are gateway not running, missing token, or policy blocking the sender.

gotchas.problem: Different DM senders are sharing the same conversation context.
gotchas.solution: Set session.dmScope to per-channel-peer (or per-account-channel-peer). Local onboarding now prefers this, but older installs may still be on shared main-session DM scope.

gotchas.problem: Telegram bot works in DM but seems blind in groups.
gotchas.solution: Disable Telegram privacy mode or make the bot a group admin, then re-add it to the group. Also verify channels.telegram.groups and requireMention/groupPolicy settings.

gotchas.problem: Discord bot is online but never sees guild messages.
gotchas.solution: Enable Message Content Intent, invite the bot with the right scopes, allowlist the guild/channel, and confirm requireMention/groupPolicy are what you expect.

gotchas.problem: Signal setup is much harder than Telegram/Discord.
gotchas.solution: That’s normal. Signal depends on signal-cli and a linked or registered account. Use a separate bot number if you want a clean bot identity and to avoid loop protection issues.
Cron runs inside the gateway, so the gateway must stay running. Check cron.enabled, verify the schedule timezone, and inspect openclaw cron list / openclaw cron runs --id <jobId>.

gotchas.problem: Memory search is empty or weak even though notes exist.
gotchas.solution: Remember memory is workspace Markdown plus an index. Run openclaw memory status, reindex with openclaw memory index, and ensure an embeddings provider is configured for memory search.

gotchas.problem: A skill shows up in list but doesn’t actually work.
gotchas.solution: Run openclaw skills check or openclaw skills info <name>. A skill can exist but be ineligible because required binaries, env vars, or platform deps are missing.

gotchas.problem: Changing config file manually caused strange runtime/auth mismatches.
gotchas.solution: Use openclaw config validate and openclaw doctor. Also check for env overrides like OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH, OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR, or macOS launchctl OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN/PASSWORD values shadowing your file.

gotchas.problem: You expected one workspace but actually launched a different profile/state dir.
gotchas.solution: Be explicit with --profile and know that OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR / OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH fully change where state lives. Mixed profiles are a common source of ‘missing sessions’ and ‘wrong token’ confusion.

cliFlags.description: Install and start the managed gateway service during onboarding.
cliFlags.useCase: Best default for an always-on assistant.

cliFlags.description: Choose onboarding depth: quickstart for sensible defaults, advanced/manual for full control.
cliFlags.useCase: Get productive fast or force the wizard to expose every option.

cliFlags.description: Select local or remote onboarding mode.
cliFlags.useCase: Attach a laptop/client to a gateway that already runs elsewhere.

cliFlags.description: Run setup commands without prompts. Useful for scripts, CI, or reproducible bootstrap flows.
Target a specific chat channel in channel-management commands.
cliFlags.useCase: Add or manage Telegram, Discord, Signal, Slack, etc.

cliFlags.description: Name a specific account instance for a channel that supports multiple accounts.
cliFlags.useCase: Run multiple bots/numbers/accounts behind one gateway.

cliFlags.description: Bind a new agent to a channel or account during agent creation.
cliFlags.useCase: Create an agent and route traffic to it in one step.

cliFlags.description: Do extra health/probe work instead of a lightweight status check.
cliFlags.useCase: Troubleshoot channels, services, and runtime state more aggressively.

cliFlags.description: Emit machine-readable output instead of terminal formatting.
Use an isolated OpenClaw profile with its own state directory.
Use dev-isolated state and shifted default ports.
cliFlags.useCase: Safely test config and channels without touching your real setup.

cliFlags.description: Create a one-shot cron job at a specific timestamp or relative time.
cliFlags.example: openclaw cron add --name "Reminder" --at "20m" --session main --system-event "Check in" --wake now

cliFlags.description: Create a recurring cron schedule using cron expression syntax.
cliFlags.example: openclaw cron add --name "Daily brief" --cron "0 7 * * *" --session isolated --message "Morning briefing" --announce

cliFlags.description: Choose whether a cron job runs in the main session or an isolated cron session.
cliFlags.useCase: Control whether scheduled work shares context or runs clean-room.

cliFlags.description: Have an isolated cron run deliver its result to a chat target instead of staying internal.
cliFlags.example: openclaw cron add --name "Status" --cron "0 7 * * *" --session isolated --message "Summarize today" --announce --channel telegram --to 123456789
cliFlags.useCase: Push scheduled reports directly to yourself.

slashCommands.description: The everyday health check from chat. Fast way to see whether the agent is reachable and what state it’s in.
slashCommands.whenToUse: First thing when something feels off.

slashCommands.description: Inspect or switch the active model. Discord also gives you an interactive model picker.
slashCommands.whenToUse: Swap between cheap/fast and strong/deep models mid-conversation.

slashCommands.description: Kick off a subagent task from chat and keep the main session clean.
slashCommands.whenToUse: Parallel research, coding, or background work.

slashCommands.description: Start an ACP-backed coding/tool harness session from chat, optionally thread-bound.
slashCommands.whenToUse: Persistent coding or tool sessions in Discord/Telegram threads.

slashCommands.description: Bind a Discord thread to a specific session/subagent target.
slashCommands.whenToUse: Turn one thread into one durable workstream.

slashCommands.description: Show active thread/session bindings and related agent context.
slashCommands.whenToUse: Inspect what a thread is currently attached to.

slashCommands.description: Write persistent config from chat if commands.config is enabled and channel configWrites permits it.
slashCommands.whenToUse: Quick remote reconfiguration without shell access.

slashCommands.description: Apply runtime-only config overrides that do not touch disk.
Resolve exec approval prompts from chat.
slashCommands.whenToUse: Approve gated shell/tool actions without going back to terminal.

slashCommands.description: Start a fresh conversational branch/session state.
slashCommands.whenToUse: When the current thread has too much baggage.

config.description: How DM sessions are isolated. main shares one DM thread; per-channel-peer isolates by sender per channel; per-account-channel-peer adds account separation too.
config.default: Historically often main; new local onboarding prefers per-channel-peer when unset
config.recommended: per-channel-peer for almost everyone; per-account-channel-peer if you run multiple channel accounts

config.description: Base tool policy profile for the assistant.
config.default: New local onboarding defaults to coding
config.recommended: coding for serious personal-agent use; tighten further for untrusted/group-facing agents

config.description: Default workspace root where AGENTS.md, SOUL.md, USER.md, MEMORY.md, and memory/ live.
config.recommended: Keep it stable and backed up; treat it as the assistant’s home directory

config.description: Controls who can DM your Telegram bot: pairing, allowlist, open, or disabled.
config.default: pairing in general docs; quickstart local onboarding may guide you toward allowlist
config.recommended: allowlist for single-owner bots, pairing if you want explicit first-contact approvals

config.description: Whether the Telegram bot should only respond when mentioned in groups/topics.
config.recommended: true for shared groups, false only in trusted/private groups

config.description: Controls guild/channel access behavior for Discord.
config.default: allowlist as the secure baseline when Discord is configured
config.recommended: allowlist, then explicitly permit only your own guild/channels

config.description: Whether Discord guild channels require @mention before the agent responds.
config.recommended: false only in private personal servers where every message should be routed to the bot

config.description: Whether native slash/menu commands are registered on supported channels.
config.recommended: Leave on auto for Discord/Telegram unless native command clutter is a problem
Enable only if you trust the channel and want remote admin from chat
Enable for power use; safer than /config because it does not persist

config.description: How often the main heartbeat runs for periodic awareness tasks.
config.recommended: 15m-30m for a personal assistant; keep HEARTBEAT.md small

config.description: Master switch for gateway scheduler jobs.
config.recommended: true if you rely on reminders or scheduled reports

config.description: Controls semantic memory indexing/search over MEMORY.md and memory/*.md.
config.default: enabled when a usable embeddings provider is available
config.recommended: Enable with a real embeddings provider and periodically check memory status/index health

config.description: Environment override for the entire mutable state directory: config, auth, sessions, cron, credentials, logs, and more.
config.recommended: Leave unset unless you deliberately want isolation or are migrating infrastructure

config.description: Environment override for the active config file path.
config.recommended: Use only intentionally; accidental overrides are a classic source of confusion

proTips: Use the dashboard first. It proves gateway + auth + model + workspace before you waste time debugging Telegram/Discord/Signal setup.
Telegram is usually the fastest real chat channel to get working; Signal is the slowest. Use that ordering unless privacy is your top constraint.
Create one agent per durable role, not per mood: coding, alerts, family, research. Routing becomes much easier to reason about.
Use openclaw agents list --bindings whenever routing feels mysterious. Most ‘wrong agent replied’ bugs are just binding precedence misunderstandings.
Memory is just Markdown plus an index. If you want the assistant to truly remember something, have it write it to MEMORY.md or memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md.
Prefer heartbeat for batch awareness checks and cron for exact-time tasks. If you schedule everything as cron, you’ll waste tokens and clutter sessions.
For multi-user or public-ish contexts, keep mention gating on and tool policy tight. OpenClaw is powerful enough that sloppy defaults become security problems fast.
Use openclaw config validate before restarting after config changes. It’s faster than discovering a typo via downtime.
If status looks wrong after an edit, suspect profile/state/env mismatch before suspecting the model. OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR and OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH can make you debug the wrong installation.
After upgrades, run openclaw doctor. It is intentionally the boring fix-everything command.
